coredata.dc:description: The dynamics of hydrogen-enriched partially premixed methane–air flames are investigated, focusing on the frequency response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations. Large-eddy simulations (LES) with conjugate heat transfer are performed on the partially premixed PRECCINSTA combustor for various methane–hydrogen mixtures, maintaining a constant thermal power and global equivalence ratio of 10 kW and 0.8, respectively. To reduce the computational cost of the LES, a three-step global mechanism is calibrated for each hydrogen-enriched partially premixed flame. The accuracy of the LES is assessed against experimental data, showing a good agreement with the mean velocity field and shape of the swirl-stabilized flame. The multiple-input–single-output structure for the flame transfer function is applied to separate the individual contributions of velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations to the overall heat release rate oscillation in the partially premixed flames. System identification with broadband forcing is used to compute the various flame transfer functions. Hydrogen enrichment significantly influences the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations, explained by the respective impact on the response of the heat of reaction, the flame speed, and the flame surface area to equivalence ratio fluctuations. The interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations is further characterized by the mixing transfer function, which describes the equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame. The higher mass diffusivity of hydrogen results in lower equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame as the frequency increases, attenuating the pronounced effect of hydrogen on the flame sensitivity to equivalence ratio fluctuations. This study contributes to the understanding of the impact of hydrogen enrichment on the thermoacoustic response of partially premixed flames.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dynamics of hydrogen-enriched partially premixed methane–air flames are investigated, focusing on the frequency response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations. Large-eddy simulations (LES) with conjugate heat transfer are performed on the partially premixed PRECCINSTA combustor for various methane–hydrogen mixtures, maintaining a constant thermal power and global equivalence ratio of 10 kW and 0.8, respectively. To reduce the computational cost of the LES, a three-step global mechanism is calibrated for each hydrogen-enriched partially premixed flame. The accuracy of the LES is assessed against experimental data, showing a good agreement with the mean velocity field and shape of the swirl-stabilized flame. The multiple-input–single-output structure for the flame transfer function is applied to separate the individual contributions of velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations to the overall heat release rate oscillation in the partially premixed flames. System identification with broadband forcing is used to compute the various flame transfer functions. Hydrogen enrichment significantly influences the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations, explained by the respective impact on the response of the heat of reaction, the flame speed, and the flame surface area to equivalence ratio fluctuations. The interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations is further characterized by the mixing transfer function, which describes the equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame. The higher mass diffusivity of hydrogen results in lower equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame as the frequency increases, attenuating the pronounced effect of hydrogen on the flame sensitivity to equivalence ratio fluctuations. This study contributes to the understanding of the impact of hydrogen enrichment on the thermoacoustic response of partially premixed flames.

ce:section.ce:para: Stringent emissions regulations have led to the wide adoption of low-emissions lean premixed gas turbines in power generation. To achieve net-zero greenhouse gas emissions, hydrogen, and hydrogen-enriched fuels are expected to play a pivotal role in facilitating the transition to a clean and sustainable low-carbon energy system. Lean-premixed gas turbines are known to be particularly susceptible to thermoacoustic instabilities. These instabilities lead to undesired consequences such as increased emissions, reduced efficiency, and even structural damage [1,2]. The effect of hydrogen enrichment on thermoacoustic instabilities depends on the combustion system, the fuel injection scheme, and the operating conditions [3–5]. Consequently, hydrogen enrichment might induce [6–8] or suppress [5,9–11] thermoacoustic instabilities.

Thermoacoustic instabilities are the result of a feedback interaction between the combustion process (heat release rate) and the acoustic field [12]. Different mechanisms of acoustics-flame interaction may lead to heat release rate fluctuations [13]. Flow disturbances, such as reactant bulk flow rate and aerodynamic fluctuations, lead to fluctuations in the flame surface area and hence the heat release rate. Various types of aerodynamic fluctuations may occur depending on the flame stabilization method [14]. In bluff-body or backplate stabilization, the flow shear layer may produce Kelvin–Helmholtz instabilities that result in vortices that wrinkle the flame surface while they are convected along the flame [15]. Vortex shedding produced in the wake of upstream elements and convected to the flame also induces heat release rate fluctuations [16,17]. In flames stabilized by a swirled flow, an additional aerodynamic fluctuation results from azimuthal velocity perturbations generated across a swirler, producing what are known as swirl number fluctuations, that are convected to the flame and lead to additional heat release rate fluctuations [18–20]. The interaction of these flow disturbances depends on the phase difference between them, controlled by the frequency, the mean flow velocity, and the position of the upstream element [17,18,20], resulting in a complex response of the flame to velocity fluctuations.

The impact of hydrogen enrichment on flame response to velocity fluctuations is closely linked to its effect on the flame shape and length [7,21]. The higher reactivity of hydrogen leads to a reduction in flame length upon hydrogen enrichment [8,22,23], which may trigger a flame stabilization transition from lifted-M shape to V shape [4,24] or from V to M shape [25–27], resulting in a more compact flame. This reduction in flame length leads to a decrease in the time delay between the flow perturbations reaching the flame and the heat release rate fluctuations [8,28,29], which directly affects the thermoacoustic stability of the system by altering the phase difference between the heat release rate fluctuations and the acoustic oscillations. Moreover, increasing the hydrogen content increases the receptivity of the flame to flow perturbations, which is reflected as an increase in the cut-off frequency [8,23]. The increase in the cut-off frequency allows the acoustics-flame interactions to occur at a wider range of frequencies, thereby making the system more susceptible to developing thermo-acoustic instabilities. However, the impact of hydrogen may change depending on the injection scheme [23]. A decrease in the cut-off frequency is observed when hydrogen is injected directly into the flame root (piloted flame), due to the redistribution of the heat release rate [30].

A fully premixed fuel and air mixture is impractical due to the required length of the mixing section and the risk of flashback. Therefore, lean premixed gas turbines are often referred to as partially premixed (or technically premixed) systems since some equivalence ratio stratification is still present at the flame. This introduces an additional mechanism of acoustic-flame interaction where equivalence ratio fluctuations induce heat release rate fluctuations. Equivalence ratio fluctuations can be a major mechanism of acoustic excitation in low-emission lean premixed combustors [31,32]. The flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations is controlled by the superposition of three effects: fluctuations of the heat of reaction, the flame speed, and the flame area [33–35]. Equivalence ratio fluctuations directly generate the first two, while the third is generated by flame speed fluctuations.

The fluctuations of the equivalence ratio are induced by acoustic oscillations at the location of the fuel injection. Fuel flow rate variations can be produced by pressure oscillations depending on the impedance of the fuel injector [36–38]. On the other hand, air flow rate variations produced by velocity fluctuations result in equivalence ratio fluctuations, even in the case of a stiff fuel injector with a constant mass flow rate. Molecular and turbulent diffusion and flow shear dispersion result in differences between the equivalence ratio fluctuations generated at the fuel injector and those convected to the flame [39–41]. This process is characterized by a mixing transfer function, also called fuel transport transfer function. Furthermore, acoustic oscillations induce unsteady mixing, where flow disturbances in the mixing section, like vortex shedding or swirl number fluctuations, affect the equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame [38,40,42]. This results in a complex interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations, even before they reach the flame.

The response of the flame to both velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations will depend on the phase difference between them when reaching the flame, leading to a frequency-dependent interaction that would be constructive when the phase difference is less than
π / 2
and destructive otherwise [20,37]. Accordingly, varying the parameters that affect the phase between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations (as the impedance or position of the fuel injector) can induce or suppress self-excited thermo-acoustics instabilities at specific frequencies [37,43].

To comprehend the impact of hydrogen enrichment on the response of partially premixed flames, it is essential to understand the individual effects of hydrogen on the various mechanisms of acoustic-flame interaction. While the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response to velocity fluctuations has been extensively studied, its effect on the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations remains largely unexplored.

In cases where the fuel injector behaves acoustically stiff and is located close to the flame, all mechanisms of acoustic-flame interaction can be combined into a single-input–single-output (SISO) flame transfer function (FTF) model [37,44]. This transfer function relates the overall heat release rate fluctuations to upstream velocity fluctuations. However, while this approach accounts for fluctuations in the equivalence ratio resulting from velocity fluctuations at the fuel injector, it does not differentiate between the individual contribution of velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations on the heat release rate fluctuation.

To separate the response of the flame to velocity fluctuations from equivalence ratio fluctuations, a fully premixed version of the partially premixed combustion system is often included in the analysis [42,45–49]. Nevertheless, this approach may not be valid if the equivalence ratio stratification alters the mean flame structure [24], resulting in a change of the flame shape and, hence the response to flow perturbations [15]. The flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations can be modeled independently using a multiple-input–single-output (MISO) structure for the flame transfer function [50,51]. This model structure considers a linear superposition of the flame response to the different types of fluctuations, which is valid for partially premixed flames when the amplitude of the fluctuations is not too high [52].

The present work contributes to understanding the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the response of partially premixed flames by studying the individual impact of hydrogen enrichment on the response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuation and their interaction in both the mixing and combustion process. The MISO approach is combined with system identification and broadband forcing [50,53] to compute the flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations of the partially premixed PRECCINSTA combustor [54]. Large eddy simulations (LES) with conjugate heat transfer are conducted for various methane–hydrogen mixtures. A global mechanism is calibrated for each mixture to reduce the computational cost of the simulations of hydrogen-enriched partially premixed flames. The results of the LES are validated against experimental data of the flame shape and velocity field. The flame transfer functions are then used to analyze the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the flame dynamics, the fuel–air mixing, and the interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations. Finally, the response of the heat of reaction, the flame speed, and the flame surface area to equivalence ratio fluctuations are also identified to explain some of the effects of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response of partially premixed flames.

The reference experiment is conducted in the PRECCINSTA atmospheric single nozzle gas turbine combustor at DLR [54,55]. A schematic diagram is presented in Fig. 1. Dry air at ambient temperature (300 K) is fed into a cylindrical plenum and conducted into a swirl generator with 12 radial swirl channels. The fuel mixture enters an annular plenum below the swirler section and then is injected through a 1 mm orifice in each swirl channel, creating a partially premixed mixture that enters the combustion chamber through a converging nozzle with an exit diameter of 27.85 mm and conical center body. The combustion chamber has a square cross-section area of 85 × 85 mm
2
and a height of 114 mm, with optical access provided by the side walls made of quartz glass held by metal posts in the corners. The hot gases exit the combustion chamber through a conical contraction followed by an exhaust duct.

The stereoscopic particle image velocimetry (sPIV) technique was used to measure the three components of the velocity ( u , v , w ) in a plane aligned with the burner center line. The instantaneous uncertainties in the in-plane velocity components ( u and v ) and the out-of-plane velocity component ( w ) were estimated to be approximately 0.7 m/s and 1.8 m/s, respectively [56]. The line-of-sight (LOS) integrated chemiluminescence from the self-excited OH* radical was imaged using an intensified high-speed camera (sample rate 10 kHz) with a UV lens and a bandpass filter (300–325 nm). The pressure in the combustion chamber was measured using a condenser microphone (sample rate 100 kHz) located at the corner, 60 mm from the dump plane. Detailed information on the experimental techniques can be found in [55,56].

A recent experimental campaign was conducted on the PRECCINSTA combustor at various operating conditions and hydrogen enrichment levels to investigate the impact of hydrogen on flame dynamics and stabilization processes [24,55,56]. The present work focused on the conditions at a constant thermal power of 10 kW and a global equivalence ratio of 0.8. Fig. 2 presents the power spectral distribution of the volume-integrated OH* chemiluminescence and the pressure in the combustion chamber for fuel mixtures with hydrogen volume fractions (
X
H 2
) ranging from 0 to 70%.

While the burner is acoustically stable for methane combustion (
X
H 2
= 0.0), high amplitude self-excited thermoacoustic instabilities develop when hydrogen volume fraction exceeds 30%. These instabilities manifest themselves as bi-modal thermoacoustic oscillations with two dominant frequencies corresponding to the 1/4 wave (bulk) mode and 3/4 wave (chamber) mode of the combustor. The frequency of these thermoacoustic modes increases with the increase of hydrogen enrichment, which is attributed to the decrease in the time delay of the flame response and the higher speed of sound in the combustion chamber due to the higher flame temperature [55,56]. A recent numerical study showed that both modes are characterized by local equivalence ratio fluctuations while velocity fluctuations, in the form of vortices generated from the backplane and the center body, interact with the flame only in the higher frequency (chamber) mode [57].

Although extensive research has been conducted on the thermoacoustic behavior of the PRECCINSTA combustor in existing literature, limited attention has been given to determining the flame transfer function, which is at the core of flame dynamics and thermoacoustic stability analysis. In order to investigate the impact of hydrogen on the flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations, large eddy simulations (LES) are performed to compute the flame transfer functions for various hydrogen enrichment levels. The experimental data is then employed as a reference and validation for the LES numerical setup.

The LES is focused on four specific operating conditions with hydrogen fuel volume fractions
X
H 2
equal to 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6. This selection encompasses pure methane and ensures a systematic increase in hydrogen enrichment for a comprehensive analysis.

The filtered multi-species Navier–Stokes equations are solved with the software Simcenter STAR-CCM+ [58]. The present work focused on the flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations as major mechanisms of acoustic-flame interaction. Therefore, the incompressible (low Mach number) formulation is used to suppress the system acoustics and get rid of the self-excited instabilities without affecting the flame response [59] since the pressure, temperature, and strain rate perturbations associated with the acoustic waves have negligible effects [60]. The solid parts of the burner are included in the LES through conjugate heat transfer formulation (CHT) to consider a proper wall temperature for the various methane–hydrogen mixtures. The air and fuel plenums are not considered in the computational domain, shown in Fig. 3(a). The fluid region is discretized with a trimmed hexahedral mesh of 12.4 M cells. A detail of the mesh with the characteristic cell size is shown in Fig. 3(b). The cell size in the flame region and the swirler is 0.25 mm, while prism layers are added to the walls to have alignment with the flow and ensure an
y
+
value around unity.

The contribution of the small turbulent scales to the stress tensor is modeled with the WALE [61] sub-grid scale model. A second-order bounded-central difference scheme [62] is used for the convective term of the momentum equation, while a second-order upwind scheme is used for enthalpy and mass species equations. For time integration, the backward time difference scheme is used, and the pressure–velocity coupling is managed with the PISO algorithm [63]. The time step is set to
3
μ s
leading to a Courant–Friedrich–Lewy number below 0.35 in the flame region and below 1.2 in the fuel injector. Mass flow inlet and total pressure outlet boundary conditions are used, and walls are treated as no-slip.

The solid regions are discretized with 2.1 M cells, with a maximum size of 3 mm and at least 5 elements in the narrow regions. The combustion chamber walls are made of quartz, while the burner, exhaust, and center bodies are considered as stainless steel with Inconel in the backplane and the center body tip. The thermal conductivity of these materials is set to 1.4, 15.0, and 13.0 W/m/K, respectively. The CHT considers wall heat loss by radiation and convection to the surroundings. Thermal emissivity ϵ of 0.67 and 0.6 are used for the quartz and the stainless steel, respectively. A constant convective heat transfer coefficient of 15 W/m
2
/K is applied to the burner body, while 100 W/m
2
/K is used for the combustion chamber and exhaust body. These values of the heat transfer coefficients and the thermal conductivity are the same as reported in [64]. To accelerate the attainment of the thermal steady-state, the heat capacity of the solid regions is reduced during the transient heating period.

The effect of radiation heat transfer in the fluid region was evaluated in the cases of pure methane and 60% hydrogen using the discrete ordinates model and gray gas emission. No significant effect was observed in the steady-state fields. Therefore, no radiation heat transfer model is included in the LES results presented below.

The combustion process is described by finite-rate chemistry, where the reaction progress rates are expressed in Arrhenius form. The species production rates are calculated by chemical integration using a stiff CVODE solver with the operator splitting algorithm [58]. Turbulence-chemistry interaction is considered using the thickened flame model [65] coupled with the Charlette efficiency function [66] and a constant coefficient
β
C h
= 0.5. A local sensor based on the spatially-filtered heat release rate is used to apply the thickening only in the flame zone. The thickening factor is locally determined based on the laminar flame thickness that depends on the equivalence ratio and the hydrogen content of the fuel mixture. The flame thickness is discretized using between nine to five grid points, corresponding to a thickening factor of around four for the various methane–hydrogen mixtures evaluated.

The species mass diffusion is modeled by the Fick’s law with constant non-unity Lewis number approach [67]. Hence, the mixture thermal conductivity and the mass diffusion coefficient of each species are computed from the viscosity using constant Prandtl and species Schmidt numbers, respectively. The Prandtl number of the mixture and the Schmidt number of each species are obtained from its average value along a 1D laminar flame for each methane–hydrogen mixture at the global equivalence ratio,
ϕ = 0 . 8
. The dynamic viscosity is computed as a function of the temperature based on the power law and the coefficients for air. The gradient model with turbulent Prandtl and Schmidt numbers equal to 0.7 approximates sub-grid energy and species mass diffusion.

The reaction of the CH
4
H
2
–air mixtures is described with a three-step global mechanism (GM) with carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H
2

This function is much simpler than alternatives proposed in the literature [71–73]. Therefore, only two additional parameters,

The target reaction rates to calibrate the three-reaction steps are computed as described in [70], using a 1D freely propagating flame (1D free-flame) with equivalence ratios ranging from 0.5 to 1.5. Once the target reaction rates are obtained, the calibration relies on Eq. (1) as an optimization problem using a genetic algorithm. Therefore, the 1D free-flame is computed only once per fuel–air mixture, yielding a fast and computationally cheap calibration method.

The detailed kinetic mechanism UC San Diego [74] is used as a reference for the calibration and one set of parameters is calibrated for each fuel mixture to have the highest accuracy in the LES. The parameters of the three-step global mechanism for the four methane–hydrogen mixtures are presented in Table 1.

The prediction of the unstretched laminar flame speed, the thermal flame thickness, and the heat of reaction with the global mechanisms (GM-3S) compared with the detailed mechanism used for the calibration (UC-SD) are presented in Fig. 4. The software Cantera [75] is used to compute the 1D free-flame, considering full multicomponent mass diffusion and Soret effect (Multi), as done for the calibration. The results from 1D free-flame simulations using the software Simcenter STAR-CCM+ with thermal and mass diffusion based on the constant non-unity Lewis approach (
Le
i
) are also included for the mixtures with 0 and 60% hydrogen.

The global mechanism can predict the values of the flame speed and the thermal flame thickness with great accuracy for the equivalence ratio ranging between 0.5 and 1.5. The mean error in the flame speed is 4% for this range. The error is higher in the rich zone when the constant non-unity Lewis approach with the coefficients for
ϕ = 0 . 8
are used. The heat of reaction is also well reproduced, ensuring a good description of the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations.

The ability of the global mechanism to describe the response of a laminar flame to stretch is assessed through simulations of a 1D symmetric counterflow premixed flame. Fig. 5 illustrates the variation of the consumption speed
S
c

S
c
due to preferential diffusion, properly predicting the maximum consumption speed and extinction strain rate. This is particularly relevant as the influence of hydrogen enrichment on the flame shape and, consequently, on the flame dynamics has been correlated with the response of the laminar flame to stretch [7,26].

To better understand the phenomena that cause thermoacoustic instabilities and to model them in acoustic analyses, it is important to determine the flame response to acoustic modulations. In the linear regime, the heat release rate fluctuations can be related to upstream velocity fluctuation using a flame transfer function as:
(4)

The flame transfer function
F
( ω )
can be referred to as an overall transfer function that incorporates all relevant mechanisms of acoustic-flame interaction in either fully premixed or partially premixed flames. For partially premixed flames, this overall transfer function is only valid for configurations with an acoustically stiff fuel injector located close to the flame compared to the acoustic wavelength (compact mixer) [37]. In a practical application, the fuel injector may not be stiff, or its stiffness could depend on the frequency of the flow acoustic fluctuations [38]; hence, the acoustics in the fuel feeding line may have a relevant impact on the thermoacoustics behavior of the system [76,77].

Furthermore, the overall transfer function in Eq. (4) does not provide information about the individual effects of velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations on the flame response, which is insufficient for the objective of the current work. The total heat release rate fluctuations can be expressed as a linear superposition of two individual transfer functions,
F

and
F

, for the flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations, respectively [50]. This leads to a multiple-input–single-output (MISO) flame transfer function as:

According to Eq. (5), all contributions associated with velocity fluctuations reaching the flame are described by

. It must be noted that in a partially premixed flame, velocity fluctuations are almost invariably accompanied by equivalence ratio fluctuations; thus, both contributions are expected in the flame response.

The flame transfer functions can be obtained separately by individual identification and subsequent reconstruction from Eqs. (4) and (5), as described in [46,49,52]. Alternatively, both transfer functions can be obtained simultaneously over a wide range of frequencies using the well-established system identification approach (SysID) with data from high-fidelity computational fluid dynamic simulations [53].

Correlation analysis [78] is used to identify the flame response to upstream velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations. The flame response is modeled by a finite impulse response in the time domain [53], which in the case of the multiple-input–single-output model, leads to [50]:

The time series data of
u
r
and
ϕ
r
are obtained from the LES by computing the area-weighted average of the axial velocity and the mass-flow-rate-weighted average of the equivalence ratio at the dump plane as the reference position. The heat release rate,
Q
̇
, is obtained by integrating over the entire volume.

In the case of the MISO model identification, it is necessary to force at the same time both the axial velocity and the equivalence ratio, ensuring two uncorrelated inputs [50]. To do so, two Daubechies wavelet-based broadband signals [79] are imposed at the air and fuel mass flow inlets, with a maximum amplitude of 15% of the mean value and a cutoff frequency of 680 Hz. In the case of the direct identification of the overall flame transfer function, one broadband signal is imposed at the air mass flow inlet, and a single-input–single-output identification is made. In this case, the equivalence ratio fluctuations at the fuel injector are only due to the variation in the air mass flow rate since the fuel mass flow rate is kept constant. Time series data for the system identification are recorded for 0.4 s after the LES reaches a statistically steady state.

This section presents the results of the steady flame without broadband forcing. The LES mean fields are computed during 0.2 s after the solid region has reached the steady-state thermal condition. Figs. 6 to 7 present the time average and root mean square profiles of the three velocity components at various locations downstream of the dump plane. The results from an initial LES (M0) using a coarser mesh in the flame region are also included to confirm that the results are independent of the mesh resolution. This mesh is the same as the one presented in Fig. 3 but with a cell size of 0.5 mm at the flame, reducing the number of cells in the fluid region by half (6.2 M). Almost identical results are obtained using both meshes for all fuel mixtures. In both cases, the fraction of the turbulent kinetic energy resolved by the mesh is over 80% for the entire domain. However, a thickening factor of around eight is needed with the lower mesh resolution, resulting in a higher contribution of the efficiency function in the combustion model. Thus, the higher flame resolution mesh is preferred to increase the fraction of the flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations resolved by the mesh.

The results of the LES are compared with the experimental data from the sPIV measurements for the cases with hydrogen fuel fractions equal to 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6. It is important to be aware that the latter two cases correspond to acoustically unstable conditions in the experiments, which may differ from the stable condition represented by the LES data.

In the case of the stable cases with
X
H 2
equal to 0.0 and 0.2 (Figs. 6 and 7), the LES predictions of the time-averaged and root-mean-square values of the three velocity components are in good agreement with the experimental data. The velocity field exhibits the characteristic features of a flame stabilized within a swirling flow, with the inflow of the gas mixture in the form of a spreading jet bounded by two recirculation zones indicated by negative values of the axial velocity v . The velocity component w shows the azimuthal velocity, i.e., the rotational part of the swirled flow, while component u shows the radial velocity. A more significant deviation from the experiments is expected for w due to the higher uncertainties in the out-of-plane component of the sPIV. In general, discrepancies in the root-mean-square values are partly attributed to the small absolute magnitude and the uncertainties of the experimental data. These discrepancies are within the range observed in the literature for LES of the PRECCINSTA combustor [64,80–82].

In cases with hydrogen fuel fractions equal to 0.4 and 0.6 (Figs. 8 and 9), the experiments present an inflow with a wider spreading angle and narrower inner recirculation zone with a higher negative axial velocity compared to LES results. This discrepancy arises from the velocity oscillations induced by self-excited thermoacoustic instabilities in the experiments under these two conditions, which lead to the change in the mean flow field. The same effects have been previously reported when comparing experimental results in the same combustor under both stable and acoustically unstable conditions [54]. Given that the incompressible LES results depict the flow fields of acoustically stable flames, the root-mean-square values of the three velocity components are consistently higher in the experiments. In these cases, the flame is subjected not only to turbulent fluctuations but also to periodic oscillations of the flow field due to thermoacoustic instabilities.

To further explore the discrepancy between LES and experimental data, the Reynolds shear stress (
v
′
u
′
¯
) is displayed in Fig. 10(a) for various hydrogen enrichment levels. The Reynolds stresses represent kinetic energy transfer from the mean flow to the fluctuating velocity field. High absolute values are associated with areas of strong turbulence. In the present case, higher values are observed within the outer shear layer between the spreading jet and the outer recirculation zone, where momentum is transferred between the high- and low-speed flow, and the highest strain rates should be encountered. Notable variations are evident between the acoustically stable and unstable cases. The kinetic energy transfer to turbulence appears to concentrate in more compact regions in the acoustically unstable cases. This is observed as a rise in peak values of shear stress and the emergence of regions with high shear stress in the central recirculation zone and along the inner shear layer.

The comparison between the sPIV and LES results is presented in Fig. 10(b). As anticipated, a good agreement is evident for the acoustically stable case, while a significant disparity is observed for the acoustically unstable case. The incompressible LES predicts a similar shear stress field for varying hydrogen additions, influenced solely by changes in flame angle and length. However, the sPIV data for the unstable case suggests that the shear stress is either reinforced or counteracted by fluctuations in the local velocity field induced by acoustic waves. This effect constitutes the primary cause of the discrepancy between LES and experimental data for cases with
X
H 2
equal to 0.4 and 0.6. In the case of pure methane, regions of slightly high shear stress in the central recirculation zone are seen in the sPIV data. This is consistent with the flow pulsation at 253 Hz observed in the experiment, as seen in Fig. 2. Although these pulsations are not strong enough to indicate instability, they may contribute to the discrepancies noted in Fig. 6 for the root-mean-square values of axial velocity in the central recirculation zone closer to the center body.

Fig. 11(a) displays the instantaneous and time-averaged heat release rate distributions for various hydrogen fuel fractions. Under the evaluated thermal power condition, the flame remains anchored to the center body for all fuel mixtures. The computed swirl number is 0.71, which slightly increases with hydrogen addition due to the increased volumetric flow rate of the fuel–air mixture. The flame is located in the inner shear layer, with its leading point at the intersection of the vortex breakdown in the inner recirculation zone and the center body. For pure methane, the flame in the outer shear layer is mostly extinguished, resulting in a predominantly V-shaped flame. As the hydrogen fraction increases, the flame stabilizes in the outer shear layer, transitioning to an M-shaped flame.

The LES results are compared with the experimental observation through the light-of-sight (LOS) of the OH*, as shown in Fig. 11(b). Although it is known that a direct quantitative relationship between OH* chemiluminescence and heat release rate can only be established for fully premixed conditions due to the non-linear response of OH* to equivalence ratio variations [83,84], OH* chemiluminescence is still a valid qualitative indicator of the heat release rate distribution. The hydrogen enrichment leads to a shorter flame, as expected. The LES results show a good agreement with the experimental data and the predicted flame shape adequately captures both flame angle and length.

The stream-wise spatial distribution of the heat release rate,
q
y
¯
= ∫ ∫
Q
̇
( x , y , z )
d x d z
, is presented in Fig. 12. The OH* profile is also included with the time-average value and the standard deviation at various axial positions for qualitative comparison with the LES results. The OH* data are normalized to have a zero far-downstream value and a volume integral of 10 kW. Differences between the LES and the experimental profile may be attributed to the non-linear relation between OH* and heat release rate for partially premixed flames. In cases with
X
H 2
equal to 0.4 and 0.6, the more significant standard deviation shows the higher fluctuation of the spatial distribution of heat release rate due to self-excited thermoacoustic instabilities.

The flame response to velocity fluctuations reaching the flame base is associated with the time delay given by the characteristic flame length [21]. The characteristic flame length is defined as the distance between the dump plane (
y = 0
) and the center of mass of the heat release rate distribution,
H
f
= ∫ y
q
y
¯
d y / ∫
q
y
¯
d y
[85]. In the case of pure methane, the heat release rate distribution is more dispersed, with the peak heat release rate occurring around 31 mm downstream of the dump plane, resulting in a flame length of 28.2 mm. As the hydrogen fraction increases, the heat release rate distribution becomes more compact and shifts upstream, with the peak heat release rate occurring closer to the dump plane. This leads to a shorter flame length of 25.9 mm, 21.7 mm, and 17.1 mm for hydrogen fractions of 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6, respectively. Consequently, the flame becomes more robust at the base near the center body tip.

Due to the fuel injection in the swirler channels, the fuel–air mixture is not fully premixed when it reaches the reaction zone, resulting in both spatial stratification and temporal fluctuation of the equivalence ratio. Therefore, the heat release occurs at different equivalence ratios along the flame front. This is shown in Fig. 13, which represents scattered data of the instantaneous temperature, equivalence ratio, and heat release rate for points with a heat release rate greater than 10% of the maximum heat release rate in the case of pure methane. The adiabatic flame temperature and the temperature at the location of the maximum heat release rate in a 1D premixed flame at each equivalence ratio are included as references. In all cases, the heat release occurs over a wide range of equivalence ratios around the global value (
ϕ = 0 . 8
). However, this range significantly increases with hydrogen enrichment, resulting in heat release at conditions going from very lean up to rich mixtures (
ϕ >
1.0).

The range of temperatures at which heat is released also increases with hydrogen enrichment. This is related to the early oxidation of hydrogen, which occurs at lower temperatures due to its lower activation energy. This early oxidation allows methane oxidation to occur under conditions of low temperature and high strain rate where pure methane combustion might not be possible [86]. This allows the flame to reside in the outer shear layer of the swirl flow producing the transition from V to M-shaped flame.

As in the case of fully premixed flames, differential diffusion of species, like H
2
, into and out of the flame front contributes to the variation of equivalence ratio in the flame [87]. However, the stratification of the equivalence ratio reaching the flame which results from incomplete premixing is the main reason for the broader range of ϕ variation when the hydrogen fraction increases. The instantaneous and time-averaged equivalence ratio distributions are shown in Fig. 14 for the cases with pure methane and 60% hydrogen and cut planes in the mixing section are used to observe the air–fuel mixing. In all cases, the fuel–air mixture exhibited stratification, with a leaner mixture near the inner wall of the mixing section and a richer mixture near the outer wall.

The fuel mixture is injected into each swirler channel as a jet-in-crossflow (JICF) and mixes with the air while being convected through the mixing section. The increase in hydrogen content in the fuel mixture results in a higher momentum flux ratio of the fuel injection expressed as [88]:
(8)

where ρ and U represent the density and bulk velocity of both the fuel jet and the air cross-flow stream. The value of J is equal to 27.7 for pure methane and increases to 31.6, 36.8, and 43.5 for hydrogen enrichment levels of 20%, 40%, and 60%, respectively. The increase in the momentum flux ratio with H
2
causes the fuel to penetrate deeper into the swirler channel, reaching the top wall earlier and promoting higher fuel stratification in the mixing section. This effect is illustrated in Fig. 15(a) through the mean equivalence ratio distribution within one of the swirler channels. The trajectory of a JICF with a fuel jet exit diameter of d according to the scaling law given by [88]:

This scaling law is valid for an unconfined JICF where the crossflow section is significantly larger than the diameter of the jet; nevertheless, it is able to qualitatively show the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the fuel injection.

These results indicate that hydrogen enrichment causes variations in the mean equivalence ratio distribution, even when the global equivalence ratio remains the same. Such variations can potentially impact the flame response to
u
′
and
ϕ
′
, as shown analytically in previous studies for equivalence ratio fluctuations [89]. This suggests that the approach of using a fully premixed condition to study the flame response of a partially premixed combustor to only velocity fluctuation becomes less valid with increasing hydrogen enrichment.

Fig. 16 shows the overall flame transfer function, as described by Eq. (4). The frequency response is shown in terms of the gain
| F |
and phase
∠ F
, with
F
( ω )

( ω )
. This transfer function indicates how the partially premixed flame responds to upstream air flow rate oscillations, where both velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations reach the flame.

Appropriate experimental data for the flame transfer function of the partially premixed PRECCINSTA burner are currently lacking. A recent attempt relies on chemiluminescence intensity to measure the heat release rate fluctuations [84], which leads to incorrect results of the flame transfer function in partially premixed flames [90,91]. This inaccuracy stems from the non-linear relationship between equivalence ratio variations and chemiluminescence intensity. Additionally, issues such as unwanted vibrations of the chamber walls have been reported [49,84].

Nevertheless, the effectiveness of the LES-SysID approach in accurately extracting the information necessary to determine the flame transfer function has been extensively validated in the literature for both SISO [59,92] and MISO approaches [50,93]. Furthermore, the current numerical setup was previously assessed for premixed flames with hydrogen enrichment [70], demonstrating that the LES-CHT-GM model produces results in good agreement with the experimental flame transfer function. In the present case, the reasonably accurate prediction of the mean flow field and flame shape reinforces the reliability of the LES results.

The results obtained by SysID with broadband forcing are compared to those obtained by sinusoidal mono-frequency forcing. For each fuel mixture, three frequencies are computed, corresponding to harmonics of the characteristic modes seen in Fig. 2. Time series of heat release rate and reference velocity data are collected for 20 periods, and Welch’s method is used to compute the standard deviation of both gain and phase. The results from SysID are in good agreement with those from mono-frequency forcing. The agreement confirms that the flame can be considered a linear time-invariant system, and SysID can be used to study its flame dynamics. This validation is important since some parts of the flame experience fluctuations around ϕ = 1.0 that could trigger non-linearities in the flame response [35].

The overall flame transfer function shows a low-frequency limit of zero gain as expected for a partially premixed flame with a stiff injector since a quasi-steady air flow rate perturbation does not change the fuel flow rate and hence the heat release rate [94]. The phase of the overall flame transfer function has a low-frequency limit of
π / 2
due to the interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations, as explained below with the mixing transfer function.

Hydrogen enrichment significantly affects the flame response, leading to an increased cut-off frequency and a more gradual phase decay, corresponding to a reduced time delay,
τ = ∠ F /
( 2 π f )
. The gain has a typical low-pass filter shape for pure methane, with a first peak followed by low gain at high frequencies. The gain at high frequencies consistently increases with hydrogen fuel fraction. For frequencies corresponding to the 1/4 wave mode of the burner (first acoustic mode in Fig. 2), the gain experiences a local minimum for all fuel mixtures. For the cases with
X
H 2
equal to 0.0 and 0.2, however, the gain is below 0.2, while for the cases with
X
H 2
equal to 0.4 and 0.6, it is larger than 0.4. These results are consistent with experimental observations where the latter two cases exhibit thermoacoustic instabilities. Nevertheless, it is the phase that plays a major role in the acoustics-flame coupling that leads to instabilities. The phase changes from around
π / 2
for
X
H 2
equal to 0.0 and 0.2 to around 0 for
X
H 2
equal to 0.4 and 0.6. These modifications are likely the factors contributing to the occurrence of the experimentally observed thermoacoustic instability.

Modulations in the gain and phase result from the interaction between the various mechanisms of acoustics-flame interaction, such as bulk flow rate, aerodynamic, and equivalence ratio fluctuations. This modulation increases with hydrogen enrichment due to the higher cut-off frequencies, as already seen for solely velocity fluctuations [16].

The flame response to velocity fluctuations
F
u
is shown in Fig. 17. This transfer function indicates the contribution of the velocity fluctuations reaching the flame to the overall heat release rate fluctuation in the partially premixed flame. The velocity fluctuations include both bulk flow rate and aerodynamic oscillations. Since a quasi-steady bulk flow rate perturbation results in a proportional variation in the supplied mixture and, consequently, the heat release rate, low-frequency limits of gain equal to one and phase equal to zero are obtained.

The gain features a maximum value at around 50 Hz, followed by modulations leading to two pronounced local minima near 120 Hz and 280 Hz. These modulations arise from the interference of various mechanisms of flow-flame interaction. In the current burner, the flame response to aerodynamic oscillations, due to the azimuthal velocity fluctuations and vortices/wakes generated downstream of the swirler, interacts with the flame response to the vortices generated at the dump plane shear layer [17]. Destructive interaction occurs when the azimuthal velocity and axial velocity fluctuations are out of phase at the flame base [19]. The phase difference between azimuthal and axial velocity fluctuations,
ψ
u − θ
, is proportional to the ratio between the distance from the swirler to the flame base
H
sw
and the convective velocity
u
b
, leading to
ψ
u − θ
= 2 π f
H
sw
/
u
b
. Fig. 17 shows that local minima occur near the frequencies where
ψ
u − θ
equals an odd multiple of π , using the mean bulk velocity in the mixing section as the convective velocity. The agreement is not perfect, as the azimuthal velocity fluctuations are not simply convected by the mean flow. Instead, they propagate as inertial waves at various velocities [95].

A decrease in the time delay and an increase in the gain at high frequencies is observed with hydrogen enrichment, as expected from previous studies. Nonetheless, this effect is less pronounced than observed for fully premixed flames in the literature, e.g. [8]. This discrepancy might be due to the influence of hydrogen on the partially premixed flame examined here, which not only shortens the flame length but also modifies the distribution of heat release rate along the flame due to the effect on the spatial distribution of equivalence ratio.

The second local minimum of the
F
u
gain nearly agrees with the frequency of the first acoustic mode of the system, indicating that the flame response to velocity fluctuations weakly contributes to the thermoacoustic instability at this frequency. This finding is consistent with a prior numerical investigation [57] in which dynamic mode decomposition analysis showed that an acoustic coupling between the flame and velocity fluctuations in the form of vortices generated at the dump plane shear layer is present in the second acoustic mode around 470 Hz, but not at the first acoustic mode around 280 Hz. Thus, the first acoustic mode is sustained mainly by coupling with equivalence ratio oscillations.

The response of the partially premixed flames to equivalence ratio fluctuations is presented in Fig. 18. Comparing with Fig. 17, it is observed that the time delay of the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations,
τ
ϕ
= ∠
F
ϕ
/
( 2 π f )
, is shorter than the time delay of the flame response to velocity fluctuations,
τ
u
= ∠
F
u
/
( 2 π f )
. This is because the fluctuations in the heat release rate produced by equivalence ratio fluctuations occur along the entire flame front, from the base to the tip [96], whereas those caused by velocity fluctuations primarily occur toward the characteristic flame length and flame tip. Hydrogen enrichment results in a higher cut-off frequency and a reduced time delay for the flame transfer function
F
ϕ
, as observed for
F
u
; however, the effect of hydrogen on the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations is more significant than its effect on the response to velocity fluctuations.

The flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations is known to be controlled by the combined effects of heat of reaction (
h
R
), flame speed (
S
L
), and flame area (
A
f
) fluctuations [33,34]. In order to gain a better understanding of the effect of hydrogen enrichment on
F
ϕ
, an attempt to identify the individual response of these three factors to equivalence ratio fluctuations from the same LES is presented in Section 5.7. The implications of the results of such an analysis are discussed here:

The flame transfer function
F
ϕ
shows a low-frequency limit equal to unity for the gain and zero for the phase. The flame response to a quasi-steady equivalence ratio fluctuation (low-frequency limit) is entirely controlled by variation in the heat of reaction since the contributions from flame speed and flame area fluctuations have equal magnitudes but opposite signs, i.e., the flame speed increases with ϕ , while the flame area decreases. Consequently, these two contributions are out of phase at low frequencies and cancel each other [35]. Note that the low-frequency limit will change if the global equivalence ratio increases, approaching stoichiometric conditions and moving toward rich mixtures due to the variation of the heat of reaction with equivalence ratio (see Fig. 4). The addition of hydrogen has a minimal impact on the volumetric heat of reaction (
ρ
h
R
), resulting in a similar gain for
F
ϕ
across different hydrogen fuel mole ratios, up to around 100 Hz in the studied flames.

As the frequency increases, the phase difference between the contributions from flame speed and flame area fluctuations decreases, and they no longer cancel each other. Consequently, the overall flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations relies on the interplay of gain and phase differences among
h
R
′
,
S
L
′
, and
A
f
′
, leading to constructive or destructive interactions.

The effect of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations is explained by three main effects. First, the increase in the sensitivity of the flame speed to the equivalence ratio (

The equivalence ratio fluctuations in partially premixed flames are generated by pressure and velocity fluctuations. Therefore, the overall flame transfer function described by Eq. (4), in the case of a stiff injector (or low Mach number) and compact mixer, can be expressed as [52]:

This transfer function is identified from the time series data

using SysID. The results are shown in Fig. 19 together with those obtained from the simulations with sinusoidal mono-frequency forcing. The equivalence ratio response to velocity fluctuations exhibits a low-frequency limit with a gain of one and a phase of π since a quasi-steady increase in the airflow rate produces a proportional decrease of the global equivalence ratio. As the frequency grows, the gain of the transfer function
H
ϕ
decreases. This is due to the diffusion and dispersion of the equivalence ratio fluctuations as they are convected through the swirler channels and the mixing section, which is enhanced by the higher spatial gradient of the higher frequency fluctuations. While hydrogen enrichment increases, the mass diffusivity of the fuel mixture also increases. This enhanced diffusion results in lower amplitude equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame and a slight decrease in the time delay with hydrogen enrichment.

The transfer function

describes both the fluctuations of the equivalence ratio at the fuel injector and the mixing process while the

(also called fuel transport transfer function), diverges from the expected convective-dissipation process in the presence of hydrodynamic fluctuations induced by acoustic velocity fluctuations. This shows that the mixing process under acoustic oscillations is controlled by two mechanisms interacting with each other. Therefore, a mixing transfer function model with two transport mechanisms was proposed [38]. A slightly modified version of this mixing transfer function is proposed here, drawing inspiration from the MISO transfer function structure of the flame response, leading to:

The interaction between these two mechanisms of the mixing transfer function produces the modulation in gain and phase of

Eq. (13) suggests that not all equivalence ratio fluctuations reaching the flame have to originate from fluctuations in the global equivalence ratio at the fuel injector. Even when hypothetically disregarding the fluctuations of the equivalence ratio at the fuel injector (
ϕ
inj

= 0), some equivalence ratio fluctuations may still reach a partially premixed flame due to unsteady mixing upstream the flame caused by aerodynamic velocity fluctuations in the presence of thermoacoustic oscillations.

The interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations, as described by the MISO model structure of the flame and mixing transfer functions, is visually represented in Fig. 20 for a simplified partially premixed combustor. The swirler represents any upstream element that interacts with the acoustics and, therefore, plays a role in the unsteady mixing. Its position relative to the fuel injector can vary.

The mixing transfer function describes the equivalence ratio fluctuations that actually reach the partially premixed flame. Consequently, it governs the interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations in the overall flame response. In Fig. 21, the flame response to the equivalence ratio fluctuations that reach the flame is presented, along with the phase difference with the flame response to velocity fluctuations,

As the frequency increases, the phase difference

The contribution of the equivalence ratio fluctuations to the heat release rate fluctuations is attenuated due to the dissipation of

Furthermore, the decrease in the time delays leads to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations to interact constructively over a broader frequency range. This results in the continuous increase in the gain of the overall flame transfer function of the partially premixed flame with the addition of hydrogen, shown in Fig. 16.

The overall flame transfer function reconstructed from Eq. (10) and presented in Fig. 16 for

This consistency validates the MISO-SysID approach and the underlying decomposition of flame-flow interaction mechanisms, even in the case with
X

= 0 . 6
, which features strong equivalence ratio stratification.

Fig. 22 presents the frequency responses of the volumetric heat of reaction, the flame speed, and the flame surface area to fluctuations in equivalence ratio reaching the dump plane. The low-frequency limit of the phase is zero for

The impact of hydrogen enrichment on the individual responses of

In the case of

However, an effect on the phase should still be observed as the flame length decreases with hydrogen enrichment, resulting in a shorter delay time. The reason for the absence of this decrease in the delay time can be attributed to the reference surface used to average the volumetric heat of reaction. In this case, the position of the iso-surface c = 0.01 does not change significantly with varying
X
H 2
. Hence, a definition of

as the reference surface would improve the results.

Nevertheless, the reconstructed flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations
F
ϕ
( ω )

F

, and
F

, shows good agreement with the flame response directly identified from the heat release rate fluctuations. This can be observed by comparing Figs. 18 to 23. This agreement supports the analysis presented for the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations. Furthermore, it lends extra validation to the MISO-SysID approach.

The present work contributes to understanding the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the frequency response of partially premixed flames through a comprehensive investigation of the individual impacts of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response to velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuation, respectively. The results are relevant for the analysis of thermoacoustic instabilities of practical combustors operating with hydrogen-enriched fuels.

Large eddy simulations of the extensively investigated PRECCINSTA combustor were conducted for various methane–hydrogen mixtures ranging from 0 to 60% hydrogen by volume. A global mechanism is calibrated for each fuel mixture to reduce the computational cost of the LES, resulting in an efficient simulation approach for hydrogen-enriched partially premixed flames. A good prediction of the mean velocity field and the flame shape compared with experimental data from sPIV and OH* chemiluminescence established the credibility of the simulation approach.

It is shown that hydrogen enrichment produces a higher stratification of the mean equivalence ratio distribution due to the higher penetration of the fuel injection in the swirler channels for the same thermal power, highlighting a typical issue encountered in adapting existing combustors for hydrogen enrichment.

The results of the flame transfer function for the partially premixed flames, computed using system identification and broadband forcing, are compared with those obtained under sinusoidal mono-frequency forcing. This comparison demonstrates that the partially premixed flame can be considered a linear time-invariant system, even in the presence of high stratification in the equivalence ratio along the flame.

The multiple-input–single-output structure for the flame transfer function was applied to compute the response of the partially premixed flames to individual velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations and their interaction. The interaction between velocity and equivalence ratio fluctuations occurs in two stages. First, in the mixing process, which controls the equivalence ratio fluctuations that finally reach the flame, and second, in the combustion process, which controls the overall heat release rate fluctuations.

The effect of hydrogen enrichment on various mechanisms involved in the dynamics of the partially premixed flame was analyzed and explained. Although the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response to velocity fluctuations has been widely studied, this is the first time, to the authors’ best knowledge, that the effect of hydrogen enrichment on the flame response to equivalence ratio fluctuations is studied. Moreover, the individual responses of the heat of reaction, the flame speed, and the flame area to equivalence ratio fluctuations were computed to get a deeper understanding of the effects of hydrogen enrichment on flame dynamics. The effect of hydrogen enrichment on the overall flame transfer function aligns with previous experimental and numerical results on acoustic stability in the same combustor.

Finally, further studies are warranted to explore the impact of alternative hydrogen enrichment approaches, such as maintaining a constant adiabatic flame temperature, laminar flame speed, or flame length.

Alex M. Garcia: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sophie Le Bras: Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jens Prager: Investigation, Software, Writing – review & editing. Isaac Boxx: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Wolfgang Polifke: Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
